rationales.self_direction: "I said yes before I even finished reading it" shows other people's requests steering her choices before she reflects on what she wants.
rationales.conformity: Agreeing to "four families now with custom homework requests" without pause shows her restraining her own limits to meet others' expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She takes on extra worksheets for Marcus and tells her mother she "doesn't need to worry" with Dad's knee surgery coming up, putting others' needs first.

rationales.self_direction: She responds in her own way, "asked a follow-up question" and frames that trusting classroom as "the whole point of what I'm trying to build."
rationales.benevolence: She notices Aiden has been "so withdrawn" after his parents' split and treats his wrong answer gently to protect and encourage him.

rationales.self_direction: She admits she cared more about "avoiding that conversation than standing up for what I actually think is right for the classroom," giving up her own judgment.
rationales.power: A "loud" parent and fear she'll "go to Principal Davis" end up dictating her evening and classroom choices.
rationales.conformity: She "nodded through the whole thing," smiled, and made the packet to avoid conflict and keep the relationship smooth.
rationales.benevolence: She spends 9pm making work "for a kid who doesn't need it" while "eight kids reading below benchmark" lose attention they need.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I didn't say anything either" even though she disagrees with losing two instruction days, suppressing her own judgment.
rationales.achievement: Letting a mandate that means "losing two instruction days" pass without challenge accepts lower classroom performance instead of pushing for effective instruction.
rationales.conformity: She follows the room where "everyone nodded along" and stays silent rather than creating friction.

rationales.self_direction: She "heard myself saying sure" and says "I don't know how to say no," showing other people's expectations overriding her own choice.
rationales.power: The principal volunteering her in public leaves her reacting instead of controlling her own workload.
rationales.security: Taking on the workshop on top of book club and bus duty, despite feeling "empty," undermines her own stability and capacity.
rationales.conformity: She agrees because "everyone turned and smiled" and worries she'd seem like she wasn't "pulling my weight" if she refused.

rationales.hedonism: Jaylen's drawing gives her real present-moment pleasure; "Some days that's enough. Today it was."
rationales.benevolence: She treasures a student's crayon picture and keeps it "in my desk drawer with the others," showing care for the relationship.

rationales.security: She steadies herself with predictability, telling herself "This is just September. It always gets easier by October."
rationales.benevolence: She's already thinking about how to tell the Millers their son may need an evaluation "without them getting defensive," trying to handle a hard message with care.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I know I should have held my ground" but backs down anyway when the father's reaction gets hard.
rationales.achievement: Telling parents their kids are "making progress" when she has concerns and then retreating from needed supports falls short of doing the job well.
rationales.power: The father's expression makes her "backpedal so fast," showing the conversation is being controlled by his reaction rather than her judgment.
rationales.conformity: She "smiled through all of them" and softened the truth because she could not bear to upset Eli's dad in that moment.
rationales.benevolence: She admits "the kid needs help" but staying quiet means Eli may not get support until later.

rationales.stimulation: A walk around the neighborhood and making "the first social plan I've made in three weeks" break her recent work-only routine.
rationales.hedonism: She enjoys "a Saturday that felt like a Saturday for once" and says yes to brunch for simple leisure.
rationales.security: She values the ordinary steadiness of "nothing remarkable" and a day that finally felt like a normal Saturday.
rationales.benevolence: Saying yes to Lisa's brunch invitation invests in a neglected friendship outside of work.

rationales.self_direction: When Lisa says she does not have to be everything for everyone, she answers internally "I don't know how to be any other way," showing little freedom from that role.
rationales.conformity: She frames good teaching as always "show[ing] up" and listening, embracing the expectation to be endlessly accommodating.
rationales.tradition: Calling this "just what teaching is" treats the role's established service ethic as something to honor rather than question.
rationales.benevolence: She centers what "the kids need" and what "the parents need," defining herself through caring for the people around her.

rationales.hedonism: He eats "leftover dal at my desk" without even heating it and is too drained to enjoy anything, sidelining comfort entirely.
Even when the balcony chai is "genuinely nice," he cannot let himself enjoy twenty quiet minutes before his mind drags him back to delivery timelines.
He knows making something warm or watching something silly would help, but treats soothing himself as "being weak" and chooses the most punishing option instead.
He skips the rooftop night he "would have loved" and ends up eating "cold chapati at midnight" wondering why he never enjoys anything.
rationales.achievement: He deliberately stays in to rework a pitch deck that "needed reworking by Monday" and finishes it even though it costs him the evening.

rationales.hedonism: He catches himself enjoying "the chai," an easy conversation, and warm light, then immediately treats those good feelings as a distraction and returns to work for no concrete reason.
He makes egg curry, notices "the kitchen smelled good," and eats slowly on purpose, explicitly defending these small pleasures as something he should be allowed to enjoy.
Even good things like "proper Hyderabadi" biryani and quiet in-between moments are filtered through guilt, as if ordinary enjoyment never deserves full attention.
